title: Array vs Linked List
description: Explain the difference between an array and a linked list. What are the trade-offs?
followUps: When would you prefer one over the other?
How does cache performance differ between the two?

solution.explanation: Arrays store elements in contiguous memory allowing O(1) random access but O(n) insertion/deletion. Linked lists use pointers to connect nodes, giving O(1) insertion/deletion at a known node but O(n) access time. Arrays are more cache-friendly; linked lists are better for frequent insertions/deletions.

title: Big-O Notation
description: What is Big-O notation? Why do we care about worst-case complexity?
followUps: What is the difference between Big-O, Big-Theta, and Big-Omega?
Why do we drop constants in Big-O?

solution.explanation: Big-O notation describes the upper bound of an algorithm's time or space complexity as input size grows. We care about worst-case because it guarantees performance under any input. Constants and lower-order terms are dropped because they become negligible for large n.

title: How Hash Tables Work
description: How does a hash table work under the hood? What causes collisions and how can they be resolved?
followUps: What is the load factor?
How does Python's dict handle collisions?

solution.explanation: A hash table uses a hash function to map keys to indices in an array. Collisions occur when two keys hash to the same index. They are resolved via chaining (linked list at each bucket) or open addressing (probing for next empty slot).

title: Stack vs Queue
description: When would you use a stack over a queue? Implement both push/pop and enqueue/dequeue.
followUps: How would you implement a queue using two stacks?
What is a deque?

solution.explanation: A stack is LIFO (last in, first out) - use when you need to reverse order or track history (e.g., undo, DFS, call stack). A queue is FIFO (first in, first out) - use when order of arrival matters (e.g., BFS, task scheduling).

title: Recursion vs Iteration
description: What is recursion? When is it preferable to iteration? Implement factorial both ways.
followUps: What is tail recursion?
How does the call stack relate to recursion?

solution.explanation: Recursion breaks a problem into smaller subproblems calling itself. It's preferable when the problem is naturally recursive (trees, graphs, divide and conquer). Iteration is more memory-efficient as it avoids call stack overhead.

title: DFS vs BFS
description: Explain the difference between DFS and BFS. When would you use BFS instead of DFS?
followUps: How does BFS find shortest paths?
What data structure does each use internally?

solution.explanation: DFS explores as deep as possible before backtracking - uses a stack. BFS explores level by level - uses a queue. Use BFS for shortest path in unweighted graphs. Use DFS for topological sort, cycle detection, or when memory is a constraint.

title: Dynamic Programming
description: What is dynamic programming? Implement Fibonacci using memoization.
followUps: What are overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure?
What is the difference between top-down and bottom-up DP?

solution.explanation: Dynamic programming solves problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results to avoid recomputation. It requires optimal substructure (optimal solution built from optimal subproblems) and overlapping subproblems.

title: Stack vs Heap Memory
description: What is the difference between stack memory and heap memory? What causes a memory leak?
followUps: What is garbage collection?
What is a pointer?

solution.explanation: Stack memory is automatically managed, fixed-size, and scoped to function calls (local variables). Heap memory is dynamically allocated, larger, and persists until freed. Memory leaks occur when heap memory is allocated but never freed/released.

testCases.expectedOutput: automatic, fixed size, fast, function scope
manual/gc, dynamic size, slower, program scope

title: Process vs Thread
description: What is the difference between a process and a thread? What is a race condition and a deadlock?
followUps: What is a mutex?
What is a semaphore?

solution.explanation: A process is an independent program with its own memory. A thread is a lightweight unit within a process sharing memory. Race conditions occur when threads access shared data concurrently without synchronisation. Deadlock occurs when two threads wait on each other indefinitely.

testCases.expectedOutput: shared memory space, lighter, can race

title: SQL vs NoSQL
description: What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases? What are ACID properties?
followUps: When would you choose NoSQL over SQL?
What is eventual consistency?

solution.explanation: SQL databases are relational with fixed schemas and ACID guarantees (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability). NoSQL databases offer flexible schemas, horizontal scalability, and often trade consistency for availability and partition tolerance (CAP theorem).

title: TCP vs UDP
description: What is the difference between TCP and UDP? When would you use each?
followUps: What happens during a TCP handshake?
What is HTTP vs HTTPS?

solution.explanation: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) guarantees delivery and ordering via acknowledgements and retransmission - used for HTTP, email, file transfer. UDP (User Datagram Protocol) is faster with no delivery guarantee - used for video streaming, gaming, DNS.

title: OOP Principles
description: What are the four pillars of OOP? Implement a class demonstrating encapsulation and inheritance.
followUps: What are SOLID principles?
What is the difference between an interface and an abstract class?

solution.explanation: The four pillars are: Encapsulation (hiding internal state), Inheritance (reusing behaviour from parent classes), Polymorphism (same interface, different implementations), Abstraction (exposing only necessary details). SOLID principles extend these for maintainable design.

title: Binary Search
description: Explain how binary search works and its requirements. Implement it.
followUps: What happens if the array is not sorted?
How would you find the leftmost or rightmost occurrence?

solution.explanation: Binary search requires a sorted array. It repeatedly halves the search space by comparing the target to the middle element. Time complexity is O(log n) because we halve the problem each step.

title: Unit Testing
description: What is unit testing? Write a function and a test for it.
followUps: What is mocking?
What is TDD (Test-Driven Development)?

solution.explanation: Unit testing verifies individual functions or components in isolation. A good unit test is fast, isolated, repeatable, and tests one thing. TDD writes tests before implementation. Mocking replaces real dependencies with controlled substitutes.

title: CAP Theorem
description: What is the CAP theorem? Explain consistency, availability, and partition tolerance.
followUps: What is eventual consistency vs strong consistency?
What is consensus in distributed systems?

solution.explanation: CAP theorem states a distributed system can only guarantee two of: Consistency (all nodes see same data), Availability (every request gets a response), Partition Tolerance (system works despite network splits). Since partitions are unavoidable, systems choose CP or AP.

testCases.expectedOutput: consistent and partition tolerant, may sacrifice availability
available and partition tolerant, may sacrifice consistency
consistent and available, cannot handle partitions
